captions.text: Having been with us since the halcyon Newgrounds  days of browser-based Flash games, that wonderful,
nostalgic era when a complete absence  of censorship was merely the means by
which we could all enjoy poo poo and dead baby  jokes and wasn't yet official government policy
towards presidential support of Neo-Nazism,  Edmund McMillen has been one of the more
consistent voices in indie development. I mean,  re-releasing the Binding of Isaac 9,000 times is
certainly consistent if nothing else. Fortunately,  he's finally broken that habit and put out an
entirely new game called Mewgenics, but if you're  frightened of new things because your mum only
fed you chicken tenders, rest assured that all the  wholesome, reliable, Flash-style cutesy animation,
poo jokes, body horror, religious references,  disquieting fixation on anuses, and child abuse
has merely been changed for Flash-style  cutesy animation, poo jokes, body horror,
religious references, disquieting fixation on  anuses, and animal abuse. Like I say, consistent.
And intellectually, I can get behind ruining cats  a lot easier than ruining abused children. 'Cause
fuck cats. One keeps sneaking into my garden  to leave poos and chew up the lawn ornaments,
and I keep just missing him when I try to catch  him in the act. So my dog tells me, anyway.
In Mewgenics, we are cast in the role of a  crazy cat lady, or rather "crazy cat faceless,
genderless, omniscient overseer," who runs a  house full of randomly generated cats jumping
around fighting, fucking, and pooing on things  depending on what furniture you've installed and
how poo receptive it is, and every few days,  we nominate four of them to go on a quest,
giving each one an adventurer class and  a silly hat before sending them off on a
succession of random encounters and isometric,  grid-based, turn-based, dis-taste-ful, tactical
battles against groups of evil cats, insects,  and McMillen's trademark indistinct, blobby
fetuses and fetus-like entities. It's a sort of  cross between Slay the Spire and X-COM in which,
when your commandos return triumphantly from the  battlefield, you get to watch them fuck and poo
on each other, but the question hanging over it  all for me is what it's all in aid of. Assuming
you don't consider watching them fuck and poo on  each other the end in itself. Binding of Isaac
sort of had a plot: you were trying to explore  the secrets of the mysterious, endless basement
and get cathartic revenge on your evil mum 90,000  times; in Mewgenics, we're not given any specific
reason why we're sending feline quartets to die  at the hands and anuses of the evil legions.
I mean, they bring back money and food to keep  your home base going, but what is the long-term
goal of the home base? What's the main villain  or threat? We're certainly not acting for the
benefit of the cats because they can't be sent  on quests more than once. If they manage to not
die and return home in glory, you're expected  to donate them to one of several extremely
suspect individuals in town in order to raise  our relationship score with them, acquire new
upgrades for the house, and make room for new  cats. We are not shown or informed what these
people are doing with our old cats, but we never  see them again and they always need new ones, so
something tells me they're not putting on fashion  parades. It's rather upsetting considering that,
much as what happens with X-COM, I get attached  to those cat units who acquire enough quirks
and abilities on their adventure for some kind of  personality to emerge. Like my hunter with several
insect familiars who wore a decaying lump of meat  on their head and was permanently grinning broadly
as if taking perverse relish in the rank stench  he no doubt emitted. I liked him. And then he
made it home, and I had to say toodle-oo and send  him to that scientist with the unusually large
stock of black plastic bin liners. I know you  don't understand human emotions, Edmund, or why
other people don't like staring at anuses all day,  but some of us make investments in these things.
Especially considering how long a single run  can take. Turn-based tactical battles don't
exactly pass at a brisk pace when you have to  micromanage everyone's movement and ballistic
farts one by one. It's slow going even when you're  not fighting bosses, with their hundreds of HP you
have to whittle down as you defend yourself  as best you can from all their bullshit,
in both the figurative and literal sense. So I'd  come home after an arduous quest to literal hell
and back long enough that I've come to love  my merry band and their artfully crafted,
overpowered builds before going, "WELP" and  boxing them all up to mail to creepy dude C
who's promised to add an extension to my  house if I give him 25 gently used cats.
Twenty-five fucking cats for the first house  extension upgrade? What do you even need that
many for? You mincing them up to make the cement?  That's at least seven runs' worth of cat veterans,
and that's if I'm not also giving cats to  the other five perverts. In other words,
making permanent progress in the game is a  rather obnoxiously long process, with most of
our achievements being completely ephemeral.  Powerful he-cats and useless wee-twats alike
come and go like it's the production line at the  suspiciously inexpensive chicken nugget factory.
Now, I'm misrepresenting things here. While  donating veteran cats is the only way to get
permanent upgrades, you don't HAVE to do it;  you need a few around to repel the occasional
invasion boss, and of course they can be used for  breeding. But in defiance of it being essentially
the eponymous feature, I didn't see much point  in trying to exert any control over the breeding;
it's entirely random if, when, who and with  what degree of sensitivity fucking takes place,
and the resultant kittens don't seem to  have a better chance of decent starting
stats or abilities than the random cats who  show up every day who've finally grown tired
of pooing in socially acceptable places  outdoors. And now we're getting to it,
all success comes down to a roll of the dice,  the traditional flaw of the roguelite; you've
got very limited control over who breeds and  in what manner your cats level up after battle,
and the path of your quest is littered with  random skill checks against random cats. You
might have three cats with intelligence  stats in the double digits but then find
an intelligence skill check being directed at  the fourth cat who won't stop licking the paint
off of walls and who the others brought along  so they could put out cigarettes on his face.
But while I'm iffy about the broad mechanics,  the vagueness of our ultimate goal, the isometric
camera in battles making it occasionally really  hard to parse what's going on when dudes are
clustered together, and the fundamentalist  approach to the teachings of RNGesus, once
I'd gotten past some initial stumbling, there's  something about Mewgenics that keeps me coming
back. I find myself properly strategizing—always  have at least one healer; try to use lots of
familiars because each one is another thing for  the enemy to poo on instead of me. I've finished
Hades 2 on my Steam deck now that Lord Moros has  finally fucking put out, so I had a vacancy for
an after-work, casual, me-time game, and somehow,  Mewgenics has wormed its way into that position,
and I'm not yet inclined to break out the  deworming tablets, as it were. It's possible
the randomness and ephemeralness has gotten its  claws in and put me in the position of mindless,
slack-jawed, pulling on the fruit machine lever  in the hope of just one more sniff at the anus
of success, but at least the visuals are appealing  and the soundtrack's good, and it's funny I should
say that because that's the exact opposite  of what one normally expects from an anus.
